What happened is in the report. How to find it faster next time isn’t.

10 min read
Just now

--

Every organization that runs production systems writes post-mortems.
The format varies. Some are detailed documents with timelines, contributing factors, and action items. Some are brief summaries in a ticketing system. Some are verbal debriefs that get partially captured in meeting notes. Some are thorough enough to satisfy a compliance requirement and shallow enough to be useless for anything else.
What almost all of them share is a specific omission.
They document what happened. They document what the fix was. They document what action items were created to prevent recurrence.
They do not document how the investigation actually went. Not the sanitized version where the team followed a logical path to the root cause. The actual version. The wrong hypothesis that was pursued for forty minutes. The question that changed the direction of the investigation and why it wasn’t asked earlier. The signal that was visible in the data thirty minutes before anyone looked for it.
That omission is the reason the same class of failure appears in post-mortems repeatedly across organizations that have been writing post-mortems for years.
The post-mortem captures the answer. It does not capture the path. And the path is the only thing that makes the next investigation faster.

What Post-Mortems Are Actually Written For
Post-mortems serve multiple purposes in most organizations and the tension between those purposes explains why they almost universally fail to capture what matters most for learning.
The first purpose is accountability. Something went wrong. Someone needs to understand what happened. The post-mortem is the artifact that demonstrates the organization took the incident seriously, investigated it thoroughly, and has a plan to prevent recurrence.
The second purpose is action item generation. The post-mortem produces a list of things to fix. Monitoring gaps. Configuration improvements. Architectural changes. Process improvements. The action items are the deliverable.
The third purpose, which most organizations claim but few actually achieve, is organizational learning. The post-mortem should make the next engineer faster at diagnosing the next similar incident.
The first two purposes are served by documenting what happened and what the fix was. The third purpose requires something different. It requires documenting the investigation itself. The wrong turns. The dead ends. The moment when the investigation changed direction and why.
That documentation is almost never written because it is uncomfortable to write.
Writing that the team spent forty minutes looking in the wrong place before asking the question that found the answer requires the team to document their own confusion. It requires naming the wrong hypothesis explicitly. It requires acknowledging that the answer was visible earlier than anyone looked for it.
Organizations that prioritize accountability and action items over learning produce post-mortems that are clean, professional, and nearly useless for making the next investigation faster.
Organizations that prioritize learning produce post-mortems that are honest about the investigation path and are therefore genuinely useful.
The difference between those two types of organizations is not sophistication. It is what the post-mortem is actually for.

The Three Questions That Are Never in the Template
Every post-mortem template I have seen includes some version of the same questions.
What happened? What was the impact? What was the root cause? What was the fix? What action items were created?
These questions produce the accountability and action item documentation. They do not produce learning.
The three questions that produce learning are almost never in the template.
The first question: what was the earliest signal that something was wrong, and when was it first visible in the data?
This question is not asking when the alert fired. Alerts fire at thresholds. The incident started before the threshold was crossed. The earliest signal is almost always visible in the metrics or logs before the alert fired, sometimes by minutes, sometimes by hours.
Identifying the earliest signal serves two purposes. It tells you how much earlier the incident could have been detected with better monitoring or alerting. And it tells you what to look for at the beginning of the next similar incident, before the alert fires, when intervention is cheapest.
Most post-mortems document when the alert fired. The earliest signal is rarely documented because finding it requires going back through the data and looking for it deliberately rather than just documenting the timeline of the incident as it was experienced.
The second question: what was the question that changed the direction of the investigation, and why wasn’t it asked earlier?
In almost every incident, there is a single question that, once asked, pointed the investigation at the right place. Before that question, the investigation was searching. After that question, the investigation was converging.
That question is almost never the first question asked. It is usually asked twenty to sixty minutes into the investigation, after the wrong directions have been exhausted.
Identifying that question explicitly and asking why it wasn’t asked first is the most valuable learning a post-mortem can produce. It reveals what mental model or framework was missing at the beginning of the investigation that would have produced the question earlier.
Most post-mortems don’t document this because the incident commander doesn’t remember the exact moment the investigation turned, or because documenting it requires acknowledging that forty minutes were spent in the wrong place.
The third question: what would I look for first if I saw this exact symptom combination again tomorrow?
This question forces the person writing the post-mortem to convert the specific incident into a reusable pattern. Not the specific root cause. The diagnostic path. The symptom combination that appeared, the question that found the root cause, and the order of checks that would find it fastest next time.
This question produces the thing that makes the next similar incident faster. Not the action item to fix the specific thing that broke. The mental model that makes future investigations faster.
Most post-mortems don’t include this because it requires thinking beyond the specific incident to the class of incidents it represents.

The Investigation Path No One Writes Down
I want to be concrete about what the investigation path looks like when it is actually documented.
Here is a real incident, documented the way most post-mortems document it.
Timeline: Alert fired at 14:32. On-call engineer joined bridge at 14:34. Root cause identified at 15:47. Fix deployed at 15:52. Service recovering at 15:55. Total duration: 83 minutes.
Root cause: Connection pool exhaustion caused by a connection leak introduced in the 13:45 deployment. A code change in the payment service opened database connections in a code path that did not properly close them under specific error conditions.
Fix: Rolled back the 13:45 deployment. Restarted the payment service to clear leaked connections.
Action items: Add connection pool metrics to primary dashboard. Configure leak detection threshold in HikariCP. Add integration test that verifies connections are closed under error conditions.
That post-mortem is complete. It documents what happened, the root cause, the fix, and reasonable action items.
It does not document what the investigation actually looked like.
Here is what the investigation actually looked like during those 83 minutes.
14:34 to 14:55: On-call engineer joins bridge. Opens payment service dashboard. Error rate is high. Errors mention database connection failures. Pages database team. Database team checks their dashboards. Database metrics look normal. On-call engineer doesn’t believe them. Continues investigating the database.
14:55 to 15:20: Senior engineer joins bridge. Asks what the database team found. Nothing wrong with the database. Senior engineer asks: what does the payment service error log say specifically? Connection pool timeout, not connection refused. Senior engineer asks: do we have connection pool metrics? No. Senior engineer asks: when was the last deployment? 13:45. That was 47 minutes before the incident started. Senior engineer asks: are there any long-running operations in the 13:45 change?
15:20 to 15:40: Engineer reviews the 13:45 code change. Finds a new error handling code path. Traces the error handling path. Identifies a branch where a connection is opened but the finally block that closes it is not reached if a specific exception is thrown.
15:40 to 15:47: Confirms the leak by correlating the connection pool exhaustion timeline with the introduction of the leaking code path. Connection pool has been draining since 13:48, three minutes after deployment.
15:47: Root cause confirmed. Rollback initiated.
The second version of this incident contains three things the first version doesn’t.
The wrong direction that was pursued for twenty-one minutes. The investigation went to the database immediately because the errors mentioned the database. The database team was correct. The investigation was in the wrong place for twenty-one minutes.
The question that changed the direction. The senior engineer asked about connection pool metrics and the specific error type. Those two questions established that the failure was in the connection management layer, not in the database. Those questions were not asked for twenty-one minutes because nobody was asking questions before looking at the database.
The earliest signal. The connection pool started draining at 13:48, forty-four minutes before the alert fired. With connection pool metrics on the primary dashboard, the leak would have been visible as a slowly declining available connection count well before exhaustion.
The first version is useful for the compliance requirement. The second version is useful for making the next similar incident take eleven minutes instead of eighty-three.

Why Organizations Keep Having the Same Incidents
The most common pattern in production engineering organizations is this: the same class of incident appears in post-mortems repeatedly over months and years.
Connection pool issues appear four times over eighteen months. Cache invalidation failures appear three times over two years. Retry storms appear twice in six months. Each incident has a post-mortem. Each post-mortem has action items. Some action items get completed. The incidents keep appearing.
The action items address the specific instance of the problem. The post-mortem does not capture the pattern.
The connection pool action item is to add connection pool metrics to the dashboard. The pattern that the action item does not capture: connection pool exhaustion looks like a database problem until you check the pool metrics. The next time connection pool exhaustion happens, even with the metrics now on the dashboard, the investigation will still go to the database first if the pattern has not been internalized.
The action item changes the tool availability. The pattern changes the investigation behavior.
Pattern capture requires the three questions that are never in the template. What was the earliest signal? What question found it? What would you look for first next time?
The answers to those questions, written down and accessible, are the difference between an organization that has the same incident four times and an organization that has it once.

The Personal Incident Journal That Changes Everything
Most engineers rely on the organizational post-mortem as their learning artifact from production incidents.
The organizational post-mortem has the problems I’ve described. It is written for accountability and action
